data.name: server.instructions
data.value:
Kontext is your long-term memory, spoken in six verbs: - Retain (store something worth remembering) - Retract (take back what should never have existed) - Recall (search by meaning) - Reclaim (fetch by id) - Recollect (browse by type/tag) - Reflect (synthesize higher-level memories from what's there)
Recall before you guess — re-deriving something you already learned is the expensive path. Retain the moment you learn something durable, written to stand on its own. To correct a memory, retain the correction with supersedes; retract only what should never have existed. Reflect periodically, when observations pile up — not on every turn.

data.name: tool.recall
data.value: Search your memory by MEANING — not reclaim (that's by id), not recollect (that's by type/tag, no ranking). Reach for this before you act on something you might already know: at the start of a task, when a name, decision, error, file, or preference comes up that you may have seen before, or any time you'd otherwise guess. Recalling is cheap; re-deriving something you already learned — or silently contradicting a past decision — is not. Results come back ranked best-first: treat the order as the system's confidence and each memory's type as how far to trust it (a firsthand OBSERVATION outranks HEARSAY picked up in conversation). Only what is currently true surfaces — retracted and superseded memories never do. Side effect worth knowing: recalling a memory keeps it salient (refreshes its recency clock), so the things you actually use stay near the top over time. Recall freely.

data.name: tool.recall.param.options
data.value: Optional knobs — limit, minimum score, tag pre-filter, include-full. Null ⇒ server defaults.

data.name: tool.recall.param.query
data.value: What you are trying to remember, in natural language — phrase it like a question to a colleague ("what did we decide about the checkpoint format?"), not keywords.

data.name: tool.reclaim
data.value: Fetch exact memories by id — not recall (that's by meaning), not recollect (that's by type/tag). Use it for progressive disclosure: when an earlier result (a recall hit, a reflection) handed you only ids and you now want the complete folded records, provenance (evidence, reasoning) included. Not a search and no ranking — you get exactly the ids you asked for, including retracted or superseded records (you asked by id, so they are not hidden here). Ids that don't exist are simply absent from the result, never an error — diff the returned ids against what you asked for to see which missed. Like recall, reclaiming refreshes the recency clock of the memories it returns.

data.name: tool.recollect
data.value: Browse your memories by structure, not relevance — not recall (that ranks by meaning), not reclaim (that's by id). Use it to enumerate or gather a filtered set — "all my PLANs tagged project::engram", "everything I retracted this week" — where you want a list, not a ranked answer to a question. The types filter is any-of (empty = any type); all supplied tags must be present. Retracted memories are never returned (they were mistakes; only reclaim by exact id still surfaces them). Ordered by sort (default: newest-retained first); reverse with direction. Read-only.

data.name: tool.reflect
data.value: Step back and synthesize. This recalls broadly over the store and writes higher-level, DERIVED memories from what it finds — a FACT, PROCEDURE, PROFILE, SUMMARY, or PLAN that cites the memories it rests on — and may supersede or retract the ones the new insight subsumes. Reflection is the operation that produces derived memories; there is no "reflection" memory type. Reach for it periodically, when a pile of observations wants to become a durable conclusion — not on every turn. Writes new memories and is destructive (may retract/supersede); long-running — expect it to complete asynchronously rather than return instantly. Not idempotent. Returns the ids it synthesized, superseded, and retracted.

data.name: tool.reflect.param.options
data.value: Optional correlation id and tag scoping. Null ⇒ the server assigns an id and reflects over everything.

data.name: tool.retain
data.value: Store something worth remembering — a decision and its reasoning, an error and its fix, a user preference, a durable fact. Reach for it the moment you learn something you'd want back later, and write each memory so it stands on its own: a reader months from now, with no other context, should understand it. Set the memory type honestly — it is the trust axis, so don't dress HEARSAY up as OBSERVATION — set importance by how much it matters, cite what the memory rests on in evidence, and list any memories it replaces in supersedes (the old ones stay readable, marked superseded; there is no separate supersede call). Additive: never overwrites or deletes. Not idempotent — each memory without a pre-set id creates a new record; a supplied id must be NEW (an id that already exists is rejected — to replace, retain the successor with supersedes). Returns one result per memory, in order, with its server-assigned id.

data.name: tool.retain.param.reconcile
data.value: Take a beat before moving on: also hand back, per stored memory, existing memories the new one looks to duplicate or contradict (nearest first) so you can supersede or retract them now. Never blocks the write — the memory is stored either way; skip it and the scheduled "dream" pass reconciles later. Default false.

data.name: tool.retract
data.value: Take back a memory that should not exist at all — it was never true, or it must be forgotten. FIRST ASK: is there a corrected memory to replace it? If yes, do NOT retract — retain the correction with supersedes instead; that is the common case ("I know better now"), keeps the old one as readable history, and the successor wins on recency. Retract is the narrow escape hatch for what supersede cannot express: wrong with no replacement (avoids inventing an anti-fact), a forget-me removal (privacy/PII), or when memories derived from this one must go with it. It CASCADES — a summary that cited this memory is retracted too, its evidence having vanished — and it is the only hide primitive: retracted memories stop surfacing in recall and recollect; only reclaim by exact id still returns them. Idempotent; nothing is physically deleted. Returns this memory's id plus everything the cascade removed.

data.name: tool.retract.param.reason
data.value: Why you're taking this back (e.g. "user corrected me", "verified false in commit abc"). Encouraged: it is the only record of why the memory is gone, it stops you relitigating it later, and it explains the cascade.

data.name: tool.retain.param.memories
data.value: One or more memories to store — each written to stand on its own.

data.name: tool.retract.param.memoryId
data.value: The memory to take back.

data.name: tool.reclaim.param.ids
data.value: The exact memory ids to fetch back.

data.name: tool.recollect.param.options
data.value: The filters and ordering — types (any-of), tags (all-of), limit, sort, direction.

data.name: tool.reflect.param.query
data.value: What to reflect on, in natural language — the theme to synthesize over.

data.name: type.Citation
data.value: One cited source. Exactly one arm is set: a cited memory, or a cited KurrentDB log record.

data.name: type.Citation+ToMemory
data.value: A citation of another memory.

data.name: type.Citation+ToMemory.memory
data.value: The cited memory.

data.name: type.Citation+ToRecord
data.value: A citation of a KurrentDB log record.

data.name: type.Citation+ToRecord.record
data.value: The cited KurrentDB log record.

data.name: type.Evidence
data.value: What a memory rests on: the reasoning that produced it together with the sources it cites. Derived memories carry it; a raw observation rests on nothing and leaves it empty. Reasoning and citations travel together — a memory carries both or neither.

data.name: type.Evidence.citations
data.value: The sources this memory cites as support, in citation order: other memories and/or KurrentDB log records. Empty for a raw observation.

data.name: type.Evidence.reasoning
data.value: The "because" that ties the cited sources to this memory: the derivation from the citations to the conclusion. Kept out of the memory's content so recall indexes the memory itself, not the citation trail. Empty for a raw observation.

data.name: type.LeanMemory
data.value: The lean recall projection: enough to use a memory and judge it, without the provenance trail (evidence, reasoning). For those, request the full body or reclaim by id.

data.name: type.LeanMemory.content
data.value: The memory text.

data.name: type.LeanMemory.importance
data.value: Its salience level.

data.name: type.LeanMemory.memoryId
data.value: Reclaim by this id for the full record.

data.name: type.LeanMemory.memoryType
data.value: The trust axis — the record's cognitive type.

data.name: type.LeanMemory.retainedAt
data.value: When the memory was retained — age, for context.

data.name: type.LeanMemory.tags
data.value: Labels carried on the memory.

data.name: type.Memory
data.value: A memory's intrinsic content — the write/command shape passed to retain. The read path returns the folded stored memory; the two shapes are deliberately separate so the write and read models evolve independently.

data.name: type.Memory.content
data.value: The memory's textual content.

data.name: type.Memory.evidence
data.value: What this memory rests on: the sources it cites and the reasoning tying them to this memory. Empty for a raw observation.

data.name: type.Memory.id
data.value: Unique identifier of the memory; optional — if absent the server generates a new one. When supplied it must be a NEW UUID (useful for idempotent retries or referencing this memory within the same batch) — an id that already exists is rejected; to replace an existing memory, retain the successor with supersedes.

data.name: type.Memory.importance
data.value: The agent's salience rating — how much this memory matters. Higher importance keeps it retrievable longer as recency fades; it does not change the decay rate. Default: Normal.

data.name: type.Memory.sentiment
data.value: The sentiment of the memory — set by the enrichment pipeline, not the agent.

data.name: type.Memory.supersedes
data.value: Memory ids this memory supersedes (replaces). The superseded ones stay readable, marked superseded. Set at retain — there is no separate supersede operation.

data.name: type.Memory.tags
data.value: Scoped or bare labels.

data.name: type.Memory.type
data.value: The record's cognitive type — Kontext's trust axis (replaces an explicit confidence field).

data.name: type.Memory.urgency
data.value: The urgency of the memory — set by the enrichment pipeline, not the agent.

data.name: type.Memory.validity
data.value: The world-time span the content refers to (perceived from the text); a point event sets start == end. Null when the text implies none.

data.name: type.MemoryImportance
data.value:
The agent's salience rating, set at retain. An additive term in ranking, not a decay control: higher importance keeps a memory's score up as recency fades, so it stays retrievable longer — it does not change the decay rate. A coarse bucket by design.
Values: - Unspecified: Not set — treated as Normal. - Low: Incidental — fine to let it fade. - Normal: The default. - High: Decisions, fixes, preferences worth keeping. - Critical: Top salience — architectural calls, hard-won lessons.

data.name: type.MemoryRef
data.value: A pointer to another memory that supports this one.

data.name: type.MemoryRef.id
data.value: The cited memory id.

data.name: type.MemoryRef.position
data.value: Its position in the log (exact, replayable).

data.name: type.MemorySentiment
data.value:
Emotional tone of a memory's content, tagged by the enrichment pipeline — not agent-set.
Values: - Unspecified: Not yet classified. - Positive: Approving / good-news tone (a fix worked, a success). - Neutral: Matter-of-fact, no clear valence. - Negative: Adverse tone (a failure, a complaint, a broken thing).

data.name: type.MemoryType
data.value:
What a memory is — Kontext's trust axis. Whether a memory is firsthand or derived is not a type: it is visible in its evidence (empty ⇒ firsthand/asserted; populated ⇒ derived, inheriting the certainty of the memories it cites). There is deliberately no "reflection" type — reflection is the process that produces the derived types.
Values: - Unspecified: Default / not set. - Observation: A perceived event — "what happened". Firsthand, so the highest trust. Episodic (decays). - Hearsay: A claim heard in dialogue — a naturally low-trust observation, kept unverified so a fabricated "fact" injected via conversation can't launder itself into truth (the "memory-hacking" guard). Episodic. - Fact: A durable truth about the world, a system, or a project. Persists; superseded by newer evidence, never decayed. - Procedure: Durable how-to: a convention, workflow, or skill. Persists, superseded, not decayed. - Profile: Durable facts about a principal — identity, role, and personal preferences (those binding only that principal). A preference binding a team/project is a Procedure/Fact at that scope, not a Profile. - Plan: A future course of action. Time-bound — valid until it's acted on or it expires. - Summary: A consolidation of other memories — a recap, digest, or index. Always derived (cites its sources as evidence), so its certainty is inherited from them. Persists, refreshed as those sources change.

data.name: type.MemoryUrgency
data.value:
How time-pressing a memory's content is, tagged by the enrichment pipeline — not agent-set.
Values: - Unspecified: Not yet classified. - Low: No time pressure — background knowledge. - Medium: Worth acting on before long. - High: Needs attention now (blocker, incident, deadline).

data.name: type.RecallOptions
data.value: The optional knobs for a recall — the query itself is a direct parameter.

data.name: type.RecallOptions.includeFull
data.value: When true, each hit carries the complete folded record instead of the lean projection. Default false: lean hits, easier on your context.

data.name: type.RecallOptions.limit
data.value: How many memories to return after ranking. Ask for a handful (5–10); asking for many more mostly dilutes the top results.

data.name: type.RecallOptions.minScore
data.value: Drop anything the server scores below this cutoff — raise it when you only want strong matches, leave it at 0 to see everything that ranked.

data.name: type.RecallOptions.queryId
data.value: Optional correlation id for this recall; null ⇒ the server assigns one. Keep it to tie later feedback or inspection back to this exact query.

data.name: type.RecallOptions.tags
data.value: Optional pre-filter: only memories carrying these tags are considered. Use it only when you already know the dimension to narrow to; empty ⇒ search everything.

data.name: type.RecallResult
data.value: Result of a recall — hits ranked best-first.

data.name: type.RecallResult.memories
data.value: The recall hits, ranked best-first.

data.name: type.RecallResult.queryId
data.value: Echoes the recall's query id (assigned by the server if it was left empty).

data.name: type.RecalledMemory
data.value: One recall hit — a ranking score plus the memory, lean by default or the full folded record when include-full was set. Exactly one body arm is set.

data.name: type.RecalledMemory+Full
data.value: The complete folded record (as reclaim returns) — present when include-full was set.

data.name: type.RecalledMemory+Full.memory
data.value: The complete folded record of the hit.

data.name: type.RecalledMemory+Lean
data.value: The default lean projection — enough to use and trust a hit.

data.name: type.RecalledMemory+Lean.memory
data.value: The lean projection of the hit.

data.name: type.RecalledMemory.score
data.value: Final ranking score (base × certainty); higher = stronger match. A low top score means nothing matched well.

data.name: type.RecollectOptions
data.value: The filters and ordering for a recollect — browse by structure (type/tags), not relevance. Recollect has no payload, so every input lives here.

data.name: type.RecollectOptions.direction
data.value: Sort direction. Default: descending.

data.name: type.RecollectOptions.limit
data.value: Max memories to return. With a limit, the sort decides which slice you get.

data.name: type.RecollectOptions.sort
data.value: How to order results. Default: newest-retained first. Reach for Importance when you want the top-N significant memories rather than the most recent.

data.name: type.RecollectOptions.tags
data.value: Optional filter: only memories carrying ALL of these tags (empty ⇒ no tag filter).

data.name: type.RecollectOptions.types
data.value: Optional filter: any-of these types (empty ⇒ any type).

data.name: type.RecollectSort
data.value:
The dimension a recollect orders its results by (there is no relevance here — that's recall).
Values: - Unspecified: Not set — defaults to RetainedAt. - RetainedAt: When the memory was recorded — chronological. The default. - LastAccessedAt: The recency clock — surfaces what has been salient (recalled) lately. - Importance: The agent's salience rating — surfaces the significant ones. Pairs well with a limit: the top-N important memories rather than arbitrary ones.

data.name: type.RecordRef
data.value: A pointer to a KurrentDB log record that supports this memory.

data.name: type.RecordRef.id
data.value: The record's id.

data.name: type.RecordRef.position
data.value: Its position in the log (exact, replayable).

data.name: type.ReflectOptions
data.value: The optional knobs for a reflect pass — the theme (query) is a direct parameter.

data.name: type.ReflectOptions.queryId
data.value: Optional correlation id for this reflection; null ⇒ the server assigns one.

data.name: type.ReflectOptions.tags
data.value: Scope the reflection to memories carrying these tags; empty ⇒ range over all.

data.name: type.ReflectResult
data.value: Result of a reflect pass: the ids it synthesized, superseded, and retracted.

data.name: type.ReflectResult.queryId
data.value: Correlates to the reflect call — supplied on the request, or server-assigned.

data.name: type.ReflectResult.retractedMemoryIds
data.value: Ids of memories retracted.

data.name: type.ReflectResult.supersededMemoryIds
data.value: Ids of memories superseded.

data.name: type.ReflectResult.synthesizedMemoryIds
data.value: Ids of memories synthesized.

data.name: type.RelatedMemory
data.value: A pre-existing memory that looks related to a just-stored one.

data.name: type.RelatedMemory.memoryId
data.value: The related, pre-existing memory.

data.name: type.RelatedMemory.similarity
data.value: Embedding cosine, ~0..1 (higher = more alike).

data.name: type.RetainResult
data.value: Result of a retain — one outcome per input memory, in the same order.

data.name: type.RetainResult.results
data.value: One result per input memory, in order.

data.name: type.RetainedMemory
data.value: The outcome for a single stored memory.

data.name: type.RetainedMemory.memoryId
data.value: The stored memory's id. Retain always stores; the related list never blocks it.

data.name: type.RetainedMemory.related
data.value: Advisory, populated only when retain was asked to reconcile: existing memories this one looks to duplicate or contradict, nearest first.

data.name: type.RetractResult
data.value: Result of a retract — the memory plus every derived memory removed by the cascade.

data.name: type.RetractResult.retractedMemoryIds
data.value: The retracted memory plus every derived memory removed by the cascade.

data.name: type.SortDirection
data.value:
Sort direction for a recollect listing.
Values: - Unspecified: Not set — defaults to Descending. - Descending: High → low: newest / most-important / most-recently-accessed first. - Ascending: Low → high: oldest / least first.

data.name: type.StoredMemory
data.value: The complete, folded read model of a single memory — every lifecycle event applied. Self-contained, for inspection and direct fetch (reclaim/recollect), not for ranked retrieval. Deliberately separate from the memory command shape so read and write models evolve independently.

data.name: type.StoredMemory.content
data.value: The memory's textual content.

data.name: type.StoredMemory.evidence
data.value: What this memory rests on: cited sources and the reasoning tying them to it. Empty for a raw observation.

data.name: type.StoredMemory.importance
data.value: The salience level set at retain (higher keeps it retrievable longer as recency fades).

data.name: type.StoredMemory.lastAccessedAt
data.value: When the memory was last accessed — the recency clock, advanced by each recall. Null if never accessed.

data.name: type.StoredMemory.memoryId
data.value: Unique identifier of the memory.

data.name: type.StoredMemory.memoryType
data.value: The record's cognitive type — Kontext's trust axis.

data.name: type.StoredMemory.retainedAt
data.value: When the memory was retained.

data.name: type.StoredMemory.retractedAt
data.value: When the memory was retracted, if it was.

data.name: type.StoredMemory.sentiment
data.value: The sentiment of the memory, set by the enrichment pipeline.

data.name: type.StoredMemory.supersededAt
data.value: When a newer memory superseded this one, if any.

data.name: type.StoredMemory.supersededBy
data.value: The memory that superseded this one; set only when superseded-at is set.

data.name: type.StoredMemory.supersedes
data.value: Memory ids this memory supersedes (replaces).

data.name: type.StoredMemory.tags
data.value: Scoped or bare labels.

data.name: type.StoredMemory.urgency
data.value: The urgency of the memory, set by the enrichment pipeline.

data.name: type.StoredMemory.validity
data.value: The world-time span the content refers to (perceived from the text).

data.name: type.Tag
data.value: An agent-agnostic filter label with an optional scope (namespace). Scoped tags let you filter on a typed dimension (e.g. project:kurrentdb) without a contract change per dimension, and the same scope can repeat on one memory. Both value and scope are sanitized to lower kebab-case, so tags are canonical.

data.name: type.Tag.scope
data.value: Optional namespace — lower kebab-case; empty ⇒ bare tag.

data.name: type.Tag.value
data.value: The label — lower kebab-case.

data.name: type.TemporalContext
type.TemporalContext.from
data.value: Start of the state, or the moment of a point event.

data.name: type.TemporalContext.to
data.value: End of the interval; null ⇒ ongoing / open-ended.
